entry.title: Update on Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance
entry.content:
Update on Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance

We are excited to announce the release of the new version of the Reference Solution Architecture for Master Data Governance (MDG), which has recently been published on Business Accelerator Hub and on the SAP Signavio Process Navigator - SAP for Me.

This updated version includes new solution variants for SAP Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02 in classic mode and in cloud-ready mode. One of the key highlights of this release is the inclusion of detailed diagrams for business partner, product, and financial domain for master data governance in classic mode. These diagrams provide a comprehensive overview of the solution architecture, making it easier for users to understand how MDG fits into their existing IT landscape and how it can be integrated with other systems and applications.  

Updates in Detail

1. Solution Variant “Federated Master Data Governance with Core Data Owner on SAP S/4HANA”

We added the information that Federated Master Data Governance can also be used with MDG on lower releases/classic-mode. More information can be found in the how-to guide and in the blog "Consumption of Federated Governance Made Easy" from @Mark63.

In the next two solution variants, we have replaced the existing one for “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA” with two new solution variants to illustrate the supported processes: 

2. Solution Variant “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA – Classic Mode”

In the Solution Value Flow Diagram, you can see that MDG on SAP S/4HANA, classic mode supports multiple domains, including business partner, product, and financials. This diagram provides a clear representation of how a business process variant is implemented and supported by a specific solution. 

As example you can see the modelled Solution Process Flow Diagrams for Decentral Creation and Decentral Change of Business Partners. In the case of Decentral Creation, a consolidation process is used, whereas for Decentral Change, the MDG, Central Governance Change Request is used. 

The descriptive text next to the diagram contains more information: It provides details about the standard delivered process template and change request types that are suitable for decentralized creation and change processes.  

3. Solution Variant “Centrally Managed Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA – Cloud-Ready Mode”

In comparison to the Solution Variant for MDG on SAP S/4HANA, classic mode you can see the Solution Value Flow Diagram for cloud-ready mode currently only contains the Business Partner Domain. 

The Solution Process Flow Diagrams were initially published last year. We have now included three new diagrams, which demonstrate the support for Mass Load, Mass Change, and Mass Deletion of Business Partner Relationships. 

Furthermore, to draw a comparison with classic mode diagrams, here you can see the modelled Solution Process Flow Diagrams for Decentral Creation and Decentral Change of Business Partners in cloud-ready mode. In both cases, consolidation processes are used. 

We believe that this latest release will be a valuable resource for organizations looking to implement or expand their MDM capabilities. By providing a clear and comprehensive overview of the solution architecture, we aim to empower users to make the most of their MDM investment and to achieve their data management goals more efficiently.  

We invite you to explore the new Reference Solution Architecture for MDM by yourself on the Business Accelerator Hub and on the SAP Signavio Process Navigator - SAP for Me.

Further information

Curious about the initially released content and about the different diagram types such as Solution Value Flow diagram, Solution Component diagram, Solution Data Flow diagram, Solution Process Flow diagram? 

  • Check the blog post “Explore Reference Solution Architecture for Master Data Governance” from my colleague @KarinBlank. 
  • Watch our Video: How to use Reference Solution Architecture for SAP Master Data Governance - YouTube 

To stay up to date, simply follow the SAP Community Topic Page for SAP Master Data Governance, join the Questions & Answers forum and Blog space, and check the MDG roadmap for all MDG versions. 

Best, Christina Lindner

entry.title: End-To-End Creation of View Cluster
entry.content:
View Cluster: 

View Cluster is a group of maintenance dialogs which are collected in maintenance unit for business or technical reasons. 

The main purpose is that view cluster allows end users to maintain the complex cross-table data and ensure consistency of the data. 

SE54 is the transaction code for creating the View Cluster. 

SM34 is the transaction code to maintain entries in View Cluster. 

Let's Create View cluster using tables SCARS, SPFLI and SFLIGHT. 

First, we need to create maintenance views for tables SCARS, SPFLI and SFLIGHT. 

Create maintainace view for SCARR. 

Step 1: Go to SE11 and create a maintainace view for SCARR. 

Step 2 : Give description and table with which you want to create maintenance view. 

Step 3: Click on view table. 

Step 4 : Click on table fields and select the fields you want. 

Step 5: Click on Maintenance status and select the option you want for access and save and activate. 

Step 6: Go to Utilities and click on table maintenance generator and create a TMG for it. 

Step 7: Provide the function group, Authorization group and maintenance screens and click on create. 

So, maintenance view is created for Scarr (Airline) Table, similarly, need to create maintenance view for SPFLI and Flight table also. 

 

Note: 1. For SPFLI while creating the Maintenance view, once you select the fields from the table you will get the screen like below. 

In the tab view Fields Give ‘S’ in the column ‘P’ for the field Carrid. 

Note: 2. While creating Maintenance view for SFLIGHT 

Follow the same procedure as before for creating maintenance view for SFLIGHT. 

In view fields in the column ‘P’ provide ‘S’ for the fields CARRID AND CONNID as below. 

Once Maintenance view for all the above tables is created Goto SE54 Tcode and create the view cluster as below. 

Steps Create View cluster in SE54. 

Step 1: Goto SE54 and click on Edit View Cluster. 

Step 2: Click on view cluster name and click on Create/Change button. 

Step 3: Provide a short description and save. 

Step 4: Double Click on Object Structure in the left side. 

Step 5: Click on the New Entries button in the toolbar menu. 

Step 6: Enter View/Table, Short text and precedes, Dep,pos,Start. 

The Dep column of the view cluster explains the details of the entry. 

R = Header Entry. 

S = Dependent entry. 

The POS column explains the sequence of the views. 

Start column explains the first view in hierarchy, which is in ZVSCARR in this case. 

Enter the data shown below and click on the Enter Key. 

Step 7: Click on enter. 

Then provide the next view name ZVSPFLI and in the Dep column of it the value of ZSPFLI is ‘S’ because it is dependent on the predecessor view ZVSCARR, and value of predecessor is ZVSCARR. 

Then again do the same procedure for ZVSFLIGHT also in Dep column of it the value of ZVSFLIGHT is ‘S’ because it is dependent on the predecessor view ZVSPFLI, and value of predecessor is ZVSPFLI. 

Step 8: Now Select each line and click on the field dependent button, a popup appears saying ‘Field Dependencies successfully created’ Confirm information popup with enter key or click green tick. 

Similarly do the same things to remaining View/table also. 

Step 9: Once it is done for all the views, click on Header entry/goback to the main screen and click activate button. 

 

 Till now we created the View Cluster. 

Maintaining View Cluster in SM34 

Step 1: Now Go to SM34 and Enter view cluster name and click on Display/Maintenance button. 

In the first screen all the existing entries of the table SCARR would appear. 

First Screen. 

Select the record in the table scarr and click on table SPFLI. 

You will get all the records related to carrid AA. 

Select particular Connid from SPFLI View and click on table Flight you will get all the entries related to selected connid as below.

entry.title: Migrating SAP Smart forms to Adobe Forms
entry.content:
SAP Smart Forms and Adobe Forms are two popular technologies for designing and generating business documents like invoices, purchase orders, and delivery notes. As organizations modernize their SAP landscapes, there is a growing need to migrate from Smart Forms to Adobe Forms to leverage advanced features, better performance, and an enhanced user experience. This document gives an idea about migration of a smart form to an interactive form. 

Why Migrate to Adobe Forms?

  1. Enhanced User Experience:
    • Adobe Forms offer dynamic layouts and interactivity (e.g., input fields, drop-downs).
  2. Modern Integration:
    • Seamless integration with Fiori apps and SAP S/4HANA.
  3. Rich Features:
    • Advanced graphics, barcodes, and accessibility features.
  4. Global Standards:
    • Support for PDF/A compliance for archiving and digital signatures.
  5. Future-Proof:
    • SAP is focusing more on Adobe Forms as a long-term solution in its product roadmap.

Migration Challenges

Migrating from Smart Forms to Adobe Forms involves technical and functional considerations:

  • Layout Redesign: Smart Forms and Adobe Forms have different layout tools and paradigms.
  • Data Binding: Adobe Forms use context nodes and data connections, requiring re-mapping of data.
  • Functional Enhancements: Migrating is an opportunity to enhance existing forms with interactive features.
  • Learning Curve: Development in Adobe Forms requires knowledge of Adobe LiveCycle Designer.

Steps to Migrate

Step1: Go to transaction code SMARTFORMS and select the smart form to be converted into print adobe form. 

 

Here the form name is ZSRS_SF_TEST. 

Step2: Go to the next screen by clicking display button. 

A text element and table are defined in this existing smart form. 

Step3: Output of the smart form ZSRS_SF_TEST is:

Step4: Now go to Utilities-àMigration--àInteractive Form-àExport in the initial screen of the smart form as shown below. 

You will get popup. 

Step5: Provide form name and interface name and click enter. 

Step6: You will get settings window as shown below. 

Select the necessary check boxes. 

Select ‘OK’ button. It asks for package and provides the package. Save it.

Step7: Now go adobe form initial screen Tcode SFP. Provide the interface name as mentioned above.  

Step8: Go to properties tab. You get the default interface type is ‘Smart forms-Compatible Interface’ as shown. 

Activate it.

Activate the interface and come out to initial screen.  

Step9: Now provide the form name mentioned above in the initial screen. 

Step10:  Click on change button and following screen appears. 

Delete the unnecessary empty folder from the context menu.

Now, 

Step11: Go to layout tab, 

Step12: Now go to properties tab, 

When you try to activate the form, it gives some error message as ‘Unknown Layout Type’.

Change the layout type to Standard Layout as shown. 

Activate the form and execute it  

Step13: The output would be

entry.title: Uploading External File in Adobe Forms
entry.content:
UPLOADING EXTERNAL FILE IN ADOBE FORMS 

Uploading External file : 

Scenario: 

A company needs to process vendor invoices in the SAP system, and as part of the Invoice Verification process, employees need to upload supporting documents (such as PDF invoices or scanned images) for audit and payment processing. These documents are often required to be attached to the invoice in SAP for review, verification, and audit purposes. 

In this scenario, Adobe Forms is used to facilitate the upload of the vendor's invoice document directly into the SAP system during the invoice verification process. 

Advantages of Uploading external file in adobe forms: 

  • Reduce Manual Errors 
  • Automatic Document Linking 
  • User-Friendly Interface 
  • Reduce paper usage 
  • Time Saving 

Disadvantages of uploading external file in adobe forms: 

  • Complexity in implementation 
  • System performance and Storage issues 
  • Limited File  type support 
  • Dependence on Adobe and SAP Versions 
  • Cost of Adobe License and Tools  

Here I'm going to upload a below Form. 

Note: File should be PDF Format.

Steps to Upload External file in Adobe forms: 

Step 1: Steps to create Interface: 

  • Goto SFP. 

  • Select the Interface radio button. 

  • Provide Interface name and click on create. 

  • Provide description , Interface type and click on save. 

  • Provide the fields based on your form. 

Step 2: Steps to create Form: 

  • Goto SFP. 

  • Select the Form radio button. 

  • Provide form name and click on create. 

  • Provide description and Interface name and click on save. 

  • Drag and Drop the fields into Adobe form area the fields which  is required for form. 

  • Click on layout for designing the form. 

  • Go to Tools in menu bar. 

  • Click Import node and select the file/template and click on open. 

  • Select the import options based on your requirement and click on Ok. 

  • The template/file is uploaded. 

  • Map the fields from your interface to your temple. 

  • Select the Data view tab in template, it will display the interface parameters. 

  • Mapping(Drag and drop) the parameters based on your field names in form. 

  • Place the parameters to the appropriate place. 
  • If you want to place/mention the same form field name or you want to apply any font styles/Formatting style you can select Object node under Palette menu. 

  • If you want to make any adjustments to fields with the help of Object you can provide. 

  • Adjust the importing/interface parameters based on form fields. 

  • Click on Save. 

  • Active the Form and click on Execute. 
  • Provide the input values and click on execute. 

  • Provide output device type as LPO1 and click on Print Preview.

entry.title: Efficient Data Migration Using CDS Views in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Data migration is an essential part of transitioning to SAP S/4HANA. CDS (Core Data Services) Views provide an efficient, declarative approach to transform and migrate data while leveraging database capabilities like filtering, aggregation, and parallel processing. This blog demonstrates a real-world scenario for data migration using CDS views, complete with code and output, along with a detailed explanation.

This blog explains how to migrate data using CDS views with complete code, an example scenario, and a step-by-step explanation of how it works.

Scenario: Migrating Sales Data

The organization has sales data in the legacy table ZSALES_OLD. The task is to:

  1. Migrate sales data within a specified date range to the target table ZSALES_NEW.
  2. Filter data dynamically using CDS views.
  3. Use AMDP to process data in chunks for optimal performance.

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Define Source and Target Tables

Legacy Table: ZSALES_OLD

This table holds the sales data to be migrated.

Sales ID Customer ID Sales Date Product ID Quantity Amount
1001CUST012024-01-05PROD00110500
1002CUST022024-01-15PROD0025250
1003CUST032024-02-01PROD003201000
Target Table: ZSALES_NEW

This table will hold the migrated and filtered sales data.

 

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Data Target Table'
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory: #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass: #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance: #ALLOWED
define table ZSALES_NEW {
  key sales_id      : abap.numc(10),
      customer_id   : abap.char(10),
      sales_date    : abap.dats,
      product_id    : abap.char(10),
      quantity      : abap.int4,
      amount        : abap.dec(15,2)
}

 

Step 2: Create CDS View for Data Transformation

CDS views simplify the extraction and transformation of data. Using parameters, the view filters data dynamically during runtime.

 

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES'
@EndUserText.label: 'CDS View for Sales Data Migration'
define view ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES
  with parameters 
    p_start_date : abap.dats,
    p_end_date   : abap.dats
  as select from ZSALES_OLD
{
  sales_id,
  customer_id,
  sales_date,
  product_id,
  quantity,
  amount
}
where sales_date between :p_start_date and :p_end_date

 

How it Works :
The CDS view uses the parameters p_start_date and p_end_date to filter rows within a specified date range. At runtime, these parameters are dynamically supplied.

Step 3: Implement AMDP for Parallel Processing

AMDP allows executing SQLScript directly in the SAP HANA database for enhanced performance. Here, it is used to process the data in chunks.

AMDP Class Definition  

 

CLASS zcl_data_migration DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES: if_amdp_marker_hdb.

    CLASS-METHODS: migrate_sales_data
      IMPORTING
        VALUE(iv_start_date) TYPE dats
        VALUE(iv_end_date)   TYPE dats
        VALUE(iv_chunk_size) TYPE int4.
ENDCLASS.

 

AMDP Class Implementation

 

CLASS zcl_data_migration IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD migrate_sales_data BY DATABASE PROCEDURE
    FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    USING ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES ZSALES_NEW.

    DECLARE v_start_date DATE := :iv_start_date;
    DECLARE v_end_date DATE := :iv_end_date;
    DECLARE v_chunk_size INT := :iv_chunk_size;
    DECLARE v_total_days INT;

    -- Calculate total days
    v_total_days := DATEDIFF(DAY, v_start_date, v_end_date) + 1;

    -- Loop through chunks
    FOR i IN 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) DO
      INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW
      SELECT *
      FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES(
        p_start_date => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size),
        p_end_date   => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1)
      );
    END FOR;

  END METHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Code Explaination

 

DECLARE v_start_date DATE := :iv_start_date;
DECLARE v_end_date DATE := :iv_end_date;
DECLARE v_chunk_size INT := :iv_chunk_size;
DECLARE v_total_days INT;

 

  • v_start_date and v_end_date: Store the start and end dates for migration. These are passed from the ABAP layer as input parameters (iv_start_date and iv_end_date).
  • v_chunk_size: Determines the size of each chunk (number of days in each batch).
  • v_total_days: Calculates the total number of days between the start and end dates.

Calculating Total Days

 

v_total_days := DATEDIFF(DAY, v_start_date, v_end_date) + 1;

 

 The DATEDIFF function calculates the number of days between v_start_date and v_end_date. Adding 1 ensures that both boundary dates are included.

Chunked Data Processing

 

FOR i IN 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) DO

 

FOR loop: Iterates over the data in chunks.

  • The range 0 .. FLOOR(v_total_days / v_chunk_size) ensures each chunk is processed.
  • FLOOR ensures the loop operates with an integer number of chunks.

Data Insertion into Target Table

 

INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW
SELECT *
FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES(
  p_start_date => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size),
  p_end_date   => ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1)
);

 

  • INSERT INTO ZSALES_NEW: Inserts data into the target table.
  • SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES: Fetches data from the CDS view based on the chunked date range.
    • ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, i * v_chunk_size): Calculates the start date for the current chunk.
    • ADD_DAYS(v_start_date, (i + 1) * v_chunk_size - 1): Calculates the end date for the current chunk.

This logic ensures that only a subset of the data (one chunk) is processed in each iteration, reducing the load on the database.

How it Works:

  1. Input Parameters:

    • The ABAP layer passes iv_start_date, iv_end_date, and iv_chunk_size as inputs to the method.

  2. Processing:

    • The method calculates the total number of days between the start and end dates.
    • It divides the data into chunks based on the specified iv_chunk_size.

  3. Execution:

    • For each chunk, the method fetches data from the CDS view for the specified date range.
    • The data is then inserted into the target table.

  4. Output:

    • The target table (ZSALES_NEW) contains the migrated data.

Advantages

  1. Scalability:

    • By processing data in chunks, the method can handle large datasets efficiently.

  2. Reuse:

    • The CDS view ZCDS_MIGRATE_SALES can be reused for other scenarios.

  3. Flexibility:

    • The chunk size (iv_chunk_size) can be adjusted dynamically based on performance requirements.

Step 4: Create an ABAP Report to Execute Migration

This report allows you to execute the migration process.

 

REPORT zsales_data_migration.

PARAMETERS: p_start_date TYPE dats,
            p_end_date   TYPE dats,
            p_chunk_size TYPE int4 DEFAULT 10.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  TRY.
      zcl_data_migration=>migrate_sales_data(
        EXPORTING
          iv_start_date = p_start_date
          iv_end_date   = p_end_date
          iv_chunk_size = p_chunk_size
      ).
      WRITE: / 'Data migration completed successfully!'.
    CATCH cx_amdp_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
      WRITE: / 'Data migration failed:', lx_error->get_text( ).
  ENDTRY.

 

Step 5: Execute and Verify Output

 

Sales ID   Customer ID   Sales Date   Product ID   Quantity   Amount
----------------------------------------------------------------------
1001       CUST01        2024-01-05  PROD001      10         500.00
1002       CUST02        2024-01-15  PROD002      5          250.00
1003       CUST03        2024-02-01  PROD003      20         1000.00

 

Conclusion

Data migration is a vital process for successful system upgrades or transitions. Using SAP technologies like CDS Views and AMDP allows for efficient, scalable, and performant data migrations. By automating the process and leveraging parallel processing, you can minimize downtime and ensure data integrity in the target system.

entry.title: End-to-End Guide to Consume RAP application to Fiori Using SAP Business Application Studio
entry.content:
To consume a RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming) application in SAP Fiori using SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), you will typically follow a series of steps involving the creation of the RAP service, exposing it as a Cloud RAP service, and integrating it into a Fiori application. Below is an end-to-end guide outlining these steps. 

 Prerequisites 

Create one RAP application (Managed/Unmanaged/Unmanaged & Save). 

Step-by-Step Guide 

 Login to your BTP account, check if there is any destination, if not Create a destination to Cloud RAP   service. 

 Login to BTP account, Click on SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). 

  Provide the credentials (BTP Credentials), Click on Continue. 

 Click on OK Create Dev Space. 

 Click on New Project from Template. 

  Select SAP Fiori generator template 

  Select the template, and click on Next 

  Select the Data source which you want to create Destination. 

  Select the System (ABAP environment on SAP BTP). 

  Here there is no ABAP Environments, it requires credentials to do this follow the bellow steps. 

  Click three dots (bellow left side), select Cloud Foundry. 

  Click on Services, and login required,  

  Select the Credentials radio button and provide your username (your BTP account mail) and Password. 

 Go back and check the destination. 

  Provide Cloud foundry Details 

  Select Cloud foundry Organization (System generated) 

  Select your Cloud foundry space. 

  Organization and space have been set successfully. 

  Refresh the page. 

 Now select the ABAP Environment and your RAP Service, then Click on Next. 

 Select the Main entity and Navigation Entity and table type as responsive, then click on next. 

 Fill in the required fields, click on finish. 

 Right click on webapp, below the folder. Click on preview application. 

 Select the start Fiori run test. 

  Perform modify operations. 

 

Conclusion 

By following this guide, you will have created and consumed a RAP service using SAP Business Application Studio for a Fiori application. This approach leverages the power of SAP Fiori’s UI5 framework, combined with the RAP model for backend development, making it possible to efficiently create modern SAP Fiori apps.

entry.content:

* this gives a not type-compatible error:

Thanks.

entry.title: Layoutoriented storage control - change/skip intermediate storage type
entry.content:
Hi all,

in our scenario we use layoutoriented storage control to transport HUs from warehouse rack to destination bin via AGV. With confirmation of first warehouse task to intermediate storage type/bin, the second WT is submitted via interface to AGV. AGV then confirms second task. This process is working fine.

In case some/or all of the AGVs are not working, there might be the need to bring the HUs manually with forklift to destination storage bin.

  • Is it possible to change the intermediate storage type in the process or skip the intermediate storage bin and directly confirm to destination storage bin via RF-device? 
  • With confirmation to intermediate bin, WT has status "WT to Subsystem". Is it somehow possible to process the WT in RF-device without changing the status (untick "WT to Subsystem") in monitor first? (or is it possible to change the status in RF?)

Thanks!

entry.title: SCIM User Deployment to IBP System, Role assignment via SAML token possible?
entry.content:
Hello,

We are using SAP IPS do deploy and authorize Users in SAP IBP.

The IBP users receive the role assignment through the SCIM deployment from the IAS, not from a SAML token that is transferred during login.
This presents us with the challenge that the search string in the IPS “aad.group.filter” becomes longer and longer the more roles there are in the IBP which need to be mapped to AD Roles, which slows down the graph query and makes administration more difficult.
Is it possible to assign roles to users via a SAML token when logging into the IBP?
In this case, we would only deploy the users to the IBP via the IPS and thus only have one role in the “aad.group.filter”.

best regards

Bastian

entry.title: Bitcoin - The new Treasury Asset
entry.content:
Over time, balance sheet valuation has become much less important than the income statement when analyzing companies - you could say that it has become irrelevant.

The Classic: Companies that have reached the break-even point pursue a strategy of decapitalizing themselves by distributing virtually all their profits to shareholders, e.g. in the form of dividends or buybacks. Before distributing excess cash, a limited amount can be invested in growth and acquisitions, but saving up or investing randomly is basically always the least attractive option from the shareholders' point of view.

The reason for this is that conservative low-risk positions like cash or treasury bills are dilutive and not worth holding. Even keeping pace with the consumer price index is not enough: Purchasing power should increase, not just keep up.

Why things are changing: The downside of this non-liquidity strategy is that it becomes unnecessarily difficult to switch to large investments such as AI or to manage crises, as we saw in the 2008 financial crisis or the 2020 pandemic. The lack of a large corporate savings position reduces flexibility and adaptability.

Outliers like Apple are not so successful because of their unusually high cash balances, they are able to hold so much cash because they are disproportionately successful. Coca Cola, for example, like many others, has negative tangible equity but can get by with this leveraged position due to its constant cash flows.

Macroeconomic conditions: Raising unstable economic factors like inflation, interest rates and geopolitical risks are making companies changing their mind.

Bitcoin is now an attractive new option for corporate treasuries: no long-term dilution, a 21 million cap - but the average return on this 15-year-old emerging asset class far outstrips the client price index. Wall Street got into bitcoin in 2024, and Blackrock has pulled off one of the most successful ETF launches in ETF history with its iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF, and institutions are buying it.

Shareholders are once again interested in building savings positions when their respective potential returns are in the double-digit annual average. Very important functionality is also Bitcoin as hedge against fiscal deficits, geopolitical risks and currency debasement. Volatility is still high, but a time horizon of more than 4+ years balances out the risk so far.

 
 

 

The First Mover: Business intelligence company MicroStrategy (MSTR) has even switched to a full Bitcoin treasury strategy and has been so successful that even AI-powered Nvidia (NVDA), with 2600% growth from the launch of its Bitcoin treasury strategy (August 2020) to today (November 2024), cannot keep up.

Who goes first: The largest or fastest growing companies have no incentive to pioneer this new, but also volatile, treasury strategy and adopt new paradigms. The more the respective industry stagnates for a forward-thinking company, the more interesting it becomes to drop the decapitalization strategy and switch to building a strong treasury savings in Bitcoin. The company's balance sheet and access to capital markets are key to this adjustment.

The market immediately reacts very positively to Bitcoin treasury announcements, which can be seen in the respective share prices. In the early stages, it is very likely that certain equity and bond managers will take notice of the company, even if the bitcoin treasury exposure is lower. Microsoft will hold a board meeting with MicroStrategy CEO Michael Saylor in December 2024 to take a look at Bitcoin treasury exposure due to shareholder demand.

Action: Game theory says that if you're a fast mover now, the potential return of a Bitcoin Treasury adoption increases with huge opportunities for the economic power of the early companies.

 

Contact at mirko.valentic@sap.com


Learn more:

Navigating the Generative AI Era: Blockchain is key for resilient cross-company communication

NFT – Digital Asset Representation on a blockchain

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay Eligibility Fields in Design Worksheet
entry.content:
Hi @xavierlegarrec 

Maybe I am doing something wrong, but it did not work the same way for me 😥 I tried with this switch, but the result is that the global eligibility rule is basically ignored.

Here’s an example: my template is set to include inactive employees, but the customer obviously doesn’t want to see employees terminated before the bonus period. My global rule specifies: include only those terminated employees with a termination date on or after 1/1/2024. With the switch turned off, I still see employees terminated in 2022 and 2023 on the worksheet—employees who are not eligible for the payout but are still included in the table.

That’s not what I want. So again, the only method that worked for me was to use the Manager Eligibility Rule to exclude them. My tests indicate that if we want to include only selected ineligible employees, the switch needs to be set to ‘false’ in combination with Manager Eligibility Rules.

entry.title: Re: Collection of String in deep entity model
entry.content:
Which OData version (V2, V4) and which stack (CAP, RAP, ...) care you using?

OData V2 does not allow collections of strings at all.

OData V4 allows collections of strings, and they are supported in CAP. Not sure about the implementation state in RAP.

entry.title: Re: SAP Private Link Service (BETA) is Available

Hi Steven,

From the error you get it seems that you pass a malformed json after the -c flag. Since you didn't pasted the exact value I cannot suggest further what might caused the malformed json, but you can use any json editor to validate the input you give.  
Other than that, the cf create-service command works fine for SAP private link service. More details on the different parameters to pass during instance creation you can find here (specific to your Azure service) - https://help.sap.com/docs/private-link/private-link1/consume-azure-services-in-sap-btp?locale=en-US

The role of a Data Engineer in the SAP area covers more than one product; the respective person needs to have a broad knowledge of the available solutions, which in this area for now are SAP BW/4HANA and SAP Datasphere. Of course, when we look at the future, Datasphere is evolving quickly. entry.title: How to enhance system generated ODATA service in SAP BW4HANA
entry.content:
Overview: This blog will guide you through the steps to enhance the system generated OData service In BW4HANA.

Prerequisites:

  • SAP BW4HANA - SAP BW Query
  • SAP BW4HANA – OData Services

In BW4HANA Odata service can be generated automatically from BW query by clicking checkbox “By Odata”, this service is then can be activated in any SAP Gateway system and consumed from there.

In case of any requirements to enhance the system generated service below steps can be followed.

Steps:

 Step 1: Generate OData service from BW Query.

Step 2: Create a project in t-code SEGW. Please remember <Project Name>_SRV is going to be the new service name, so please name the project accordingly.

Step 3: Right click on the Data Model and click on “Redefine” -> “OData Service (SAP GW)

Step 4: In this screen it will ask for a service where we need to select the system generated service that needs to be enhanced and click “Next”

Step 5: Click Select all and click on Finish

Step 6: Enhance the Result set with new field and Save.

Step 7: Right click on project and click on “Generate Runtime”

Runtime artifacts will be generated like shown below

Step 8: Go to DPC_EXT (Data provider extended class)

Select GET_ENTITYSET method and click on Redefine button

Step 9: Write the logic to populate the newly added field here. Super class get_entityset code will be shown by default, start the new logic after the default code. Please note that this code will be executed for each and every row in output.

Step 10: Test the output of OData service.

Go to TCODE /IWFND/GW_CLIENT, give the URL in Request URI and click on execute.

Validate the output with written logic.

entry.title: Efficient Mass-Enrichment of Business Partners into SAP MDG: A Comprehensive Guide
entry.content:
What happens when you need to repeat this process 100,000 times for all of your business partners? Do you enter 100,000 addresses, choose the best match 100,000 times, download the data 100,000 times manually, and create 100,000 change requests?

At this scale, an easy process quickly becomes impractical, likely overwhelming your master data department’s resources for months. And by the time they’re finished, the data may have already changed, forcing the process to start all over again.

So, the question is: How can the mass-loading of business partners be made more efficient?

Data enrichment of large volumes of business partner master data into SAP MDG is critical for organizations managing existing business partner bases or integrating new acquisitions. The CDQ-SAP partnership addresses this challenge with a specialized mass-enrichment feature that allows organizations to efficiently load, validate, and enrich data from trusted sources.

This article outlines the process of mass-loading trusted business partner data into SAP MDG, leveraging CDQ’s data integration to ensure high data quality, reduce manual intervention, and maintain up-to-date records.

What Exactly Is Trusted External Data?

In most countries, businesses must register with local authorities to legally operate, trade, employ workers, or market their services. These registers are the primary source of truth for a company's name, tax obligations, operational locations, and legal structure. Any changes must be officially updated in these registers. The types of registers vary by country: some have both legal and tax registers, some have national registers, while others, particularly federations, maintain separate registers for each state. Despite these differences, nearly all registers share a common challenge: they each have their own data standards, interfaces, and access methods.

Integrating data from these diverse registers can be overwhelming. Even for large organizations, managing integrations with each register is often impractical. This is where CDQ comes in. CDQ integrates both official registers and commercial data providers, mapping their various data fields into a unified standard format. This approach allows businesses to access critical data seamlessly, without the need for a massive, resource-intensive integration project. SAP connects to CDQ's service, embedding this streamlined access directly into their software solutions.

Business Partner Master Data: Mass Enrichment into MDG

When uploading large volumes of trusted data into SAP MDG, handling each record manually isn’t sufficient. SAP and CDQ have developed a mass-enrichment process designed to manage mass loads effectively, whether onboarding new data from acquired entities or updating existing records. CDQ connects to trusted data sources while SAP MDG handles the software side, providing an out-of-the-box solution that minimizes the need for extensive customization.

Key Takeaways

  • Scalability: Mass-enriching addresses the limitations of manual single-record updates, making large-scale data loads efficient and manageable.
  • Data Integrity: The process includes quality checks and data enrichment steps to ensure records are up-to-date and accurate.
  • Ease of Use: The solution is designed for minimal technical support, enabling teams to handle mass loads independently.

Process Overview

SAP's Cloud Ready Mode includes robust Master Data Management (MDM) processes that enable centralized, high-quality, and governed data across the organization. MDM ensures accurate, consistent data across systems, improves data quality through validation and deduplication, and supports compliance with audit trails and change tracking. MDM also facilitates data synchronization and integration with business processes, enhancing analytics and decision-making.

In this article, we want to look at the “SAP_BP_COA SAP: Consolidate BP Automated” process, which is designed to enhance the information available about business partner master data in SAP MDG. It serves two main purposes:

  1. Onboarding New Business Partners: When a new business partner is added, this process enriches the initial data, ensuring that all relevant information is included from the start. This can include details like contact information, business addresses, and any other important information related to the partner.
  2. Updating Existing Business Partners: For business partners that are already in the system, this process ensures their data is kept up to date. This is especially useful when information changes, such as a company name, location, or other key details, which helps maintain data accuracy and relevance.

An important aspect of this process is that it doesn’t rely on a universal or global identification system to track these partners. Instead, it uses whatever identification numbers already available for each partner (like tax IDs, company registration numbers, or any unique ID the partner has) to identify and enrich their records. This makes it flexible and adaptable to various data sources without requiring new or complex ID systems.

This process handles the complete load of business partner master data for both Initial Loads and Data Updates, making it ideal for supporting Master Data migration projects, ongoing data updates, and the import of business partner master data in mergers and acquisitions (M&A) projects, where integrating data from new entities is critical.

1. Initial Check

The initial check is crucial for setting a solid foundation for the data upload process. This step begins with uploading business partner records using a standardized Excel template. Often, bulk data imports contain diverse data issues such as duplicate records, incomplete entries, internal comments, and outdated information. The initial check process automatically reviews each record, identifying and flagging any missing or inconsistent data fields.

For example, the initial check verifies if all necessary fields are present and identifies where data is missing. It focuses on the quality and format of the input data to ensure consistency and completeness. By ensuring that critical fields like addresses, tax numbers, and company names are properly filled, the initial check helps lay the groundwork for a more effective mass-enrichment process. Only records with all required information proceed, reducing errors and ensuring accurate data quality from the start.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Quality: Prevents common data issues from affecting later steps in the mass-enrichment process.
  • Efficiency: Cleansed input data ensures that resources are not wasted on unnecessary corrections down the line.

2. Reference Data Lookup

This step performs a detailed look-up against external reference databases to identify essential information like tax numbers, registration identifiers, and other legal markers for each business partner. Here, CDQ connects with a variety of trusted data sources based on your chosen configuration. Depending on the sourcing strategy, data may be retrieved from global providers like D&B or Moody’s, free government registers, or specialized data collectors such as the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) database.

For instance, a German subsidiary of a multinational corporation might be listed in both local German business registers and in a global database with Moody’s. The Reference Data Lookup can cross-reference identifiers across these sources, ensuring that each entry aligns with official records. This validation is vital for detecting duplicate or erroneous entries that may otherwise go unnoticed.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Accuracy: Cross-checks with authoritative sources improve data accuracy and reliability.
  • Flexible Sourcing: Adaptable to your preferred sources, whether international, regional, or industry-specific.

3. Address Data Validation

During this step, the system verifies and updates address information to ensure alignment with current data from trusted external sources. Often, addresses in a company’s internal records can become outdated as businesses relocate, merge, or undergo other structural changes. The Address Data Validation step matches existing records with the latest official data, allowing users to revalidate and, if necessary, rematch each record.

For example, suppose a company record lists an address that hasn't been updated in years. Through validation, MDG identifies that the company has relocated to a new address or changed its legal structure. The record can then be updated in bulk, ensuring that your address data reflects the most current information. This process can be applied to individual records or to entire data sets, giving users the flexibility to clean up old data systematically.

Key Takeaways

  • Up-to-Date Records: Maintains accurate address data to support contact and regulatory compliance.
  • Improved Data Governance: Reduces data decay by systematically updating outdated entries.

4. Matching and Match Group Filtering

In this step, each business partner record in your input file matched with records from CDQ’s trusted data sources. Here’s how it works:

  1. Initial Data Transfer: The process starts by taking key information from each master data record, including the partner's name, address, and any available identifiers (such as tax IDs or registration numbers). This information is sent to CDQ for processing.
  2. Golden Record Creation: CDQ then applies advanced algorithms to create a "golden record" for each entity. This golden record represents the best and most accurate version of the entity, combining information from multiple sources to ensure completeness and reliability.
  3. Returning the Best Match: After creating the golden record, CDQ identifies the best possible match from its trusted sources. It sends back the best candidate record, enriched with the most up-to-date and relevant data, so you have the highest quality information available for each business partner.

This matching process also includes a Match Group Filtering feature. By grouping records based on match quality, it lets users filter results to focus on the most relevant matches and avoid enriching records of inactive entities. This is particularly helpful because many registers flag businesses that are no longer operational, allowing the system to handle such cases efficiently.

For example, during this step, a company with multiple branches might appear as separate entries in various registers, each associated with a slightly different set of data points. MDG can group these records and allow you to select only the active, relevant records for further enrichment. This customization helps avoid overloading your system with unnecessary data and keeps inactive or irrelevant records out of the active database.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Governance: Ensures only active and relevant entities are kept for further processing.
  • Match Customization: Fine-tunes matching criteria to maintain data relevance and quality.

5. Best Record Calculation, Derivation, and Validation

At this stage, CDQ's "golden record" technology aggregates data from multiple sources to generate a comprehensive record that combines the most accurate and relevant information. By chaining identifiers from trusted sources, CDQ creates an enhanced record that pulls in data from various official registers and commercial providers, if available.

For example, if a company is listed in both the official German Commercial Register and D&B with slight variations in registered office addresses or other details, CDQ’s golden record technology will combine these inputs to produce a unified and complete record. This “best of all worlds” approach creates a robust record that minimizes data gaps, allowing organizations to include additional identifiers such as LEIs to strengthen data consistency across international jurisdictions.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Enrichment: Consolidates multiple sources to create a highly detailed, unified record.
  • Real-Time Updates: Pulls data from active sources, ensuring accuracy without relying on outdated static files.

6. Activation

The final activation step prepares enriched records for deployment by allowing users to perform a final quality assessment. SAP MDG summarizes any missing elements or data inconsistencies for each enriched record, providing an overview of potential issues and allowing for a final review. Users can drill down into individual records to examine data points in detail or refine search parameters and enrichment settings if certain records need adjustments.

For instance, suppose the data from a particular register is missing a crucial identifier for a small subset of records. You may choose to manually investigate or rerun specific steps for just those records to ensure complete accuracy. Once satisfied, the enriched data can be activated, replacing outdated records and integrating seamlessly with SAP MDG’s data governance framework.

Key Takeaways

  • Final Quality Assurance: Ensures the activated data meets established quality benchmarks.
  • Flexible Activation: Offers control over which records to activate, allowing for targeted improvements in data governance.

Conclusion

The SAP MDG mass-enrichment feature, in partnership with CDQ’s data integration capabilities, transforms the efficiency of business partner Business partner master data management. By standardizing the approach, SAP and CDQ put the experience of decades of master data projects into a software- and data foundation that can now be used by MDG user organizations. SAP is laying the foundation for an efficiency-gain in large master data projects. With access to a range of reliable international data sources, this solution empowers organizations to implement tailored data sourcing strategies without vendor lock-in. The combination of data quality, flexibility, and ease of use makes this process a valuable asset for both initial data loads and ongoing data updates. Explore our other articles to learn more about leveraging SAP MDG and CDQ for business partner master data Management.

Related Articles:

  • Mass enrichment of business partners with Tax and ID numbers found using the CDQ data provider
  • Hands-on with Free Tiers: CDQ Trusted Business Partner Data in SAP Master Data Governance
  • Trusted Business Partner Data in SAP Master Data Governance, cloud edition

entry.title: Understanding SAP Fiori Architecture: A Guide to its Three Key Layers
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Fiori is a modern UX framework designed to simplify and streamline user interactions with SAP systems. It offers a consistent and intuitive user experience across devices, ensuring that users can seamlessly access critical business applications from any platform. At the core of Fiori have a robust architecture that ensures reliable performance and seamless integration with SAP’s backend systems. In this blog, we'll take a closer look at the SAP Fiori Architecture, diving into its three primary layers: the Client Layer, Application Layer, and Database Layer. Each layer has distinct responsibilities and contributes to the smooth functioning of Fiori applications, enabling businesses to unlock the full potential of SAP's ecosystem.

The entire SAP Fiori Architecture is divided in three layers:

  • Client Layer which consists of the SAP Fiori Launchpad  and SAP UI5 App. For e.g.: a launchpad for various SAP UI5 application
  • Application Layer which consists of mainly OData services which is essential for communication between UI and other backend system such as S4/HANA system
  • Database Layer to store all the data given by the user or system. An example of the database layer is SAP HANA

We can focus on each layer and learn about various components provided by SAP Fiori Architecture as follows:

 

Client Layer:

As mentioned, before its purpose is to provide an interface where the user can interact with the Fiori apps. Following are the components in it.

  • Client Devices: These are the devices from which our SAP Fiori apps will be accessed. These could be smartphones, tablets or desktop browsers. We need to make sure that our app is responsive across all the platforms and can be used without any prior installation.
  • SAP Fiori Launchpad: The SAP Fiori Launchpad, typically hosted on SAP Business Technology Platform, acts as a centralized hub for all Fiori applications. Based on the roles configured in BTP, users can access various applications through this launchpad, providing a streamlined entry point for all SAP Fiori applications

 



Application Layer:

Its main purpose is to process user request, host UI components and manager varies services and communicate with the database layer

  • SAP Fiori Front-end Server: It hosts the UI components that are needed to run our SAP UI5 apps. Apart from that it also helps with other process such as authentication, routing and caching for faster access to application.
  • SAP Gateway: It allows the connection between the front-end and back-end by exposing various OData services. ODATA is the standard way to expose backend data to our Fiori application
  • SAP Fiori Apps Repository: It is a central Fiori repository which stores and manages all the FIORI application on SAP BTP cloud and provides across for cloud deployment. There is multiple pre-configured Fiori application available on it from SAP
  • SAP BTP: It is a management service which allows access to all the services for maintenance of roles, development spaces, various services that are used across the SAP eco-system.
  • SAP Web Dispatcher and Cloud Connector: Cloud Connector or dispatcher helps us to connect with various on-premises system without exposing the port to internet. It acts as reverse proxy which secures the to and from of data from Fiori application and backend systems
  • SAP BAS: It’s a development studio which can be used to extend standard fiori application or for development of custom SAP UI5 application for certain requirements. It comes as subscription in SAP BTP.

 

Database Layer:

The main purpose of this layer as explained before is to store, maintain and process business data which can be accessed via the application layer.

  • Database: It is used to store business data for e.g.: various sales data, records, etc. which will be accessed by the user. E.g. of such system is SAP HANA.
  • Back-end Systems: This is the core SAP system which contains the main business logic such as the amount and type of data that needs to be exposed for user via the UI5 app. The OData services are created to communicate between back-end systems and Fiori application. E.g. of such system is SAP S/4HANA.

 

 

Conclusion:

SAP Fiori’s layered architecture is pivotal for its flexibility, security, and efficiency. By distributing responsibilities across the Client, Application, and Database layers, Fiori provides a modular and scalable framework that aligns with the dynamic needs of businesses today. This architecture not only supports responsive design and seamless integration but also promotes the secure handling of data and resources. With SAP Fiori, companies can ensure a cohesive and optimized user experience across devices, empowering users to interact with complex SAP data in an intuitive and productive way. As Fiori continues to evolve, it stands to transform the way organizations interact with SAP, fostering a more accessible and efficient digital workspace.

entry.title: A Step by Step Guide for ICS Integration.
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, I know this is very basic and most of you all would be aware about the process but still when I tried to do for my client I did hit a few roadblocks, so I thought why not make it easy for someone new and doing this for the first time.

So first what is ICS? Basically ICS integration with SAP Concur gives a seamless data exchange between SAP'S ERP systems and Concur. Once this integration is enabled it becomes very easy and straightforward to manage expense reports posting to SAP along with master data, company's org structure details like company code and cost center etc. We all have had our troubles with reconciling the SAE extracts if they fail to post right? I bet you you have because it is such a manual process to manually correct the details and send the file again, but with SAP ICS enabled everything is just a click away.

End of the day SAP ICS integration with Concur creates a more efficient, accurate and user-friendly expense management process for organizations using SAP solutions.

Now coming to how to get this integration done with Concur and SAP's ERP solution that we are using?

Lets first see what all are required from our end. Assuming everyone to be client partners working on implementation or supporting the clients for AMS work, we should have unrestricted access to the Concur system.

Once we have that, lets go through the step by step process to activate this integration.

Step 1:

Go to Home->Administration->Authentication Admin

This will take you to the Authentication Admin page where you will see 2 options.

  1. Company Request Token

     2.OAuth 2.0 Application Management

We will first click on OAuth 2.0 Application Management.

OAuth 2.0 is a framework which enables applications to access resources with each other in a secure way i.e we don't need to give away our username and password to every application we connect Concur with instead we can enable certain permissions to access only the data each system needs.

So this page lets us to create an application to which we are going to connect Concur with by providing details like App name, App Type, App description.

Then comes the part where we allow certain Grants/Scopes like password, client credentials, refresh token etc.

And after this is where we can allow what all data can be accessed in the Allowed Scopes section. You can get the complete list of scopes from this very clear link SAP Concur Developer Center | Learn More About Scopes.

Then there is a check box which we need to check if we are planning to create an SAP ICS Application. Make sure you check that.

Once we have completed this step and click on Submit, we will be given the Client ID , Client Secret and Company UUID.

These three strings are important which will be used later but for now we just need to copy them and keep it in a word document or a notepad.

Step 2: 

Click on Company Request Token.

So this Request token is nothing but a key which is issued by Concur which needs to be given to the external application when it wants to connect with Concur using OAuth 2.0.

To generate a Request token, click on Company Request Token and enter the client id which you had saved from the previous step in the App name field and click on submit. Please note that each Request token generated will last only for 24hrs. If the connection is not established by then we have to request for a new Request token.

Now the Concur part is completed and we need to work on the SAP part.

Step 3:

Log in to the SAP application and use the T-Code CTE_SETUP

Under CTE_SETUP, Click on System->Create Connection

After this there is a step by step process where we need to give few details as seen below, (since it contains client information I have masked out the details) and click on next

Now comes the part where we need to enter the information we obtained from Concur which we saved in a notepad. And select the use Request Token option and give continue and verify the details in the next page and then complete the process.

When we complete following things happen.

  • The system generates the RFC connection.
  • The credentials are saved to the secure store and are required to refresh the access token.

Step 4:

Check Connection.

Now that we have established the connection it is time to check if the connection is complete.

For this use the below navigation.

T-Code CTE_SETUP > System > Check Connection

You should see a screen as below.

Step 5:

Set up the the cost object structure as per our Concur list management for Cost center, WBS and Internal Order.

 

 

Step 6 :

Setting up the Financial posting import.

Now in the next page select document type TE and check IBCP credit card to enable credit card payments.

Provide the other details like cost object mapping and keep clicking on next. Now when you are in the Attachments screen, Select “All documents of expense report”. In Store attachments using Select “ArchiveLink” >  “content Repository = AM Test Invoices in nscale” and add manually “Global Document Type = CTE_DOCS”.

In the Automatic Import Section, choose the type you want whether it is manual or automatic and Job Name CTE_FIN_FEEDBACK_LOOP 

Before clicking on complete validate the details we had given in each field and make sure they are correct.

Once you click on complete ICS connection is established between Concur and SAP.

Step 7:

While creating group configurations for new countries make sure to check the financial 

So there it is a step by step guide to make this integration an easier process for any newcomer with required details. I wanted to do this because I did not get something like this online so anyone who find themselves here going through these steps in the future I hope it did help you or at least gave a good idea on how to enable ICS integration.

Thanks a lot Everyone!

entry.title: SAP IDOC : Step by Step with Practical Example with Definition, Structure, Types, Format & Tables
entry.content:
Requirement

  • Customer will provide the employee data in the file.
  • Migrate employee data into SAP system using BAPI with IDOC interface.
  • Creation - BAPI
  • Creation of IDOC interface for BAPI
  • Migrating the employee data using Business Object Method

 BAPI  and BAPI Creation

B - Business   A - Application   P - Programming    I - Interface

BAPI is a standard Programming interface that enables external access to the business process in sap system

And also

BAPI is a function module which contain  a business process.

Creation of Table

Creation of Structure

Creation of Function module(BAPI)

Create a function group

Prerequisites for BAPI

  • The function module name must start with <namespace>BAPI_<Business object>_<Method> - zbapi_employee_create_02
  • All parameter(Input/Output…etc) associated type must begin with <namespace>BAPI
  • All parameters must be pass by value.
  • It should contain  RTEURN of type 'BAPIRET2' as EXPORT Parameter.
  • Function module must be RFC-enables.
  • Function module must be released. (GOTO->Release)

 

 

Create a message class and define the messages as below

For the source code put the below code.

 

FUNCTION zbapi_employee_create_02.

*"----------------------------------------------------------------------

*"*"Local Interface:

*"  IMPORTING

*"     VALUE(PEMPLOYEE) TYPE  ZBAPI_EMPLOYEE_CREATE_01

*"  EXPORTING

*"     VALUE(RETURN) TYPE  BAPIRET2

*"----------------------------------------------------------------------



  

TYPES : BEGIN OF ty_emp,

            employee_id   TYPE ztb_emp_02-employee_id,

            employee_name TYPE ztb_emp_02-employee_name,

          END OF ty_emp.



  DATA : ls_emp TYPE ty_emp,

         lt_emp TYPE TABLE OF ty_emp.



  ls_emp-employee_id = pemployee-eid.

  ls_emp-employee_name = pemployee-ename.



  IF sy-subrc = 0.

    return-type = 'S'.

    return-id = 'ZMSG_02'.

    return-number = '000'.

    return-message_v1 = pemployee-eid.

  ELSE.

    return-type = 'E'.

    return-id = 'ZMSG_02'.

    return-number = '001'.

    return-message_v1 = pemployee-eid.

  ENDIF.



ENDFUNCTION.

 

Enable RFC

Release the Function module

SAP GUI menu bar > Function Module > Release

Creation Business Object of the function module

SWO1 - Business object builder

Create object type - ZEMPOBJ02

Object type -  Internal identification of business object. EX : If someone wants to identify the business object in within SAP system itself.

Super Type - To inherit the component of existing object type. If already have an existing object type, and want to reuse it , can use super type

Object name - This is used for external identification of the business object. Using this type external system can interact with SAP system. For best practice use the business object name for this. This name is already be in case-sensitive - EMPLOYEE2

Description - It is a brief description of the business object type.

Program - Program that contain the ABAP code of the business object.

Application - The application component to which component to which the business object belongs to. Select the 'Cross-Application'.

Then we have to bind BAPI(Function Module) with Business object.

TCODE (SWO1)  > SAP GUI menu bar > Utilities > API Methods > Add method

While creating the method, the system asks for the method name for the BAPI and business object connection. For best practice you can give method name as BAPI (Function module).

Now still the BAPI is not ready for the testing. Because it's depend upon the status. There are 4 types of status.

  1. Modeled - Whenever you are defining the business object and whenever you are defining the component(Interface, Attributes, Methods) of the business object at that time the status is modeled.
  2. Implemented - Whenever the business object / business object components  have got fully functionality implemented at that time the status is implemented. This can be used for internal purpose(within SAP) and for testing purpose.
  3. Released - Once fully implemented business object / business object components released the external application can access. And after released only entry will be in BAPI explorer.
  4. Obsolete - Can't be used.

We can easily check the status as shown in below.

SWO1 > Double click on  business object component >  ABAP

As of now  business object is modeled. We have to do the internal testing. So we have to set the business object as IMPLEMENTED.

STEPS.

Put the cursor on object > Click on Edit > Change Release Status > Click on Implemented

After this we can see the Test/Execute button.

entry.title: Essential Resources for SAP Fiori Development
entry.content:
Essential Resources for SAP Fiori Development

Hello everyone, as an SAP Fiori developer, I like to stay informed of the latest features, be technically at ease, follow development and user interface best practices, or find help when I get stuck on a problem. I'm sharing below a list of resources and websites I use daily to help me master SAP Fiori development and stay up to date (feel free to add to it in the comments with your recommendations 😉 ).

SAP Official Resources

No journey into SAP Fiori development is complete without exploring the wealth of official resources SAP provides. These resources ensure you get accurate, up-to-date information on the platform’s tools, techniques, and methodologies.

SAP Fiori Design Guidelines

This official guide covers SAP Fiori UX principles, including visual design, interaction patterns, and branding guidelines. It's crucial for developers to understand the Fiori design philosophy and ensure that the apps they develop align with SAP’s standards. These guidelines are updated regularly, reflecting evolving best practices and new SAP Fiori features.

SAP Developer Center

A central hub for all things related to SAP development. It hosts a variety of tutorials, blogs, sample projects, and developer tools with examples for novice, intermediate, and advanced developers.

SAPUI5 SDK

The documentation of SAPUI5, the framework behind SAP Fiori apps. It provides detailed API documentation, code samples, and interactive tutorials to help developers understand and use SAPUI5 effectively. The SDK also includes best practices, demo applications...

SAP Community

As one of the largest communities of SAP professionals, it is filled with interactive content including developer blogs and insights, forums for troubleshooting and peer support, SAP Fiori-focused events and webinars… You can search the community to find solutions to common development problems, get advice on the latest trends, and engage with other developers who share their experiences and knowledge.

For those looking for fun and community-driven engagement, SAP Developer Challenges are a great way to test your skills while competing with fellow developers. 

SAP Help Portal

The official documentation for all SAP products, including SAP Fiori. Whether you're working on an implementation, configuring an app, or troubleshooting issues, the Help Portal has comprehensive guidelines, API documentation, and step-by-step instructions. 

SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

This library catalogs all standard  Fiori apps, offering detailed descriptions, implementation prerequisites, and links to relevant documentation. You can also find UI5 versions and determine which apps are customizable or extensible to meet your specific needs.

Online Learning Platforms

As Fiori development is a specialized field, self-paced learning platforms can provide structured paths to accelerate your expertise. Here are some of the best online resources for learning SAP Fiori and SAPUI5:

SAP Learning Hub

For more comprehensive learning and certification preparation, it is an excellent platform that provides access to vast training content. Whether you’re a beginner or a seasoned developer, the platform offers structured learning journeys tailored to specific roles and technologies. In addition to courses, you get access to workshops, certification preparation materials, and practice exams.

SAP Certification Hub

Earning an official SAP certification is a great way to demonstrate your proficiency as a developer. As a Fiori developer, you can't miss out on SAP Certified Development Associate - SAP Fiori Application Developer certification (which I'm preparing right now 😁) By achieving certification, you prove your expertise and increase your marketability in the SAP ecosystem.

Other Fun Communities and Learning Resources

Here are some more fun and interactive resources for your learning journey:

  • Stack Overflow - SAP Fiori: Find answers to specific development challenges related to SAP Fiori, with detailed discussions on common issues, troubleshooting tips, and optimization strategies.
  • Reddit - r/SAP: Informal discussions, news, and updates on SAP technologies, including Fiori development.
  • SAP Fiori YouTube Channels: Explore YouTube channels like DJ Adams one to see live coding sessions, tips, and tricks for working with SAP Fiori, UI5, and SAP Cloud Platform. His videos are an excellent way to stay updated with the latest SAP trends, and they provide practical demonstrations of development techniques.

Conclusion

I hope this short list will help you find ways to improve your SAP Fiori skills and answer any questions you may have. Don't hesitate to share your resources, tips and experiences with the community in the comments, and above all... stay curious! 👀🚀

entry.title: Must know tricks for GRC (Access Control / Process Control) or Security Consultants
entry.content:
In this article, I aim to simplify this process by providing insights into identifying SPRO node technical details. Based on my experience, I’ve observed that many users struggle to compile a complete list of the T-codes maintained under SPRO nodes. Before diving into the technical details, let’s first review some critical challenges in enterprise risk management and access control.

 

Key challenges in S/4HANA Role Design

  1. Excessive Access:
  1. Misunderstanding Reporting T-Codes:
    It is common for T-codes starting with S_ALR* or S_* to be misinterpreted as simple display reports. However, this assumption is incorrect, as many of these T-codes grant significant operational capabilities.
  2. SM30 (Table Maintenance View):
    This customizing object allows for the maintenance of values in authorization objects. If improperly restricted, it can introduce risks due to its range-based value maintenance capability.
  1. Role design issue with specific SPRO activity:

 

SPRO activities are commonly used to setup up the system and perform project specific customising settings. However, it’s often good practice to restrict the access per specific node.

 

An Opportunity to improve SAP GRC Access Control Ruleset:

  1. Critical Action and Permission Checks:
    While designing the ruleset, organisations often overlook key configurations maintained exclusively through SPRO and its relevant maintenance views. These configurations are linked to SPRO nodes that utilize specific T-codes, which may be inadvertently assigned to users through roles.

Solution:
To identify the relevant maintenance views linked to SPRO nodes, you can refer to the table CUS_IMGACH. This table provides direct access to details about SPRO nodes and their associated T-codes.

 

 

 

SOX and Financial Control Documentation

Documenting SOX or financial controls becomes significantly easier when you can identify the relevant T-codes calling maintenance views. These views provide insights into the underlying tables, enabling more efficient and accurate documentation. Below is a step-by-step approach:

Steps to Extract Relevant Maintenance Views:

  1. Download the SPRO Node List from CUS_IMGACH:
    • Export the list into an Excel file for further analysis.

 

  1. Extract the T-code List from Table USOBT_C:
    • Use a transaction like SE16 to download the relevant T-code data.

 

 

 

  1. Match Maintenance Views with VLOOKUP in Excel:
  2. Perform a VLOOKUP with sheet (1. Download the SPRO Node List from CUS_IMGACH) to correlate T-codes with their corresponding maintenance views.
    • Note: Maintenance views typically have a prefix “V_” added to the table name.

 

However, if you have still not able to find the details then copy the view name and refer the details below per below screenshots:

 

  1. Verify Details in SE11:
      • Select the "View" radio button. In this example I have chosen view “V_T052” which is mainly used to maintain details for “Terms of Payment”

 

      • Enter the maintenance view or table name associated with the SPRO node’s T-code.

 

 

 

On left hand side under tab Table/Join Conditions you may find the details of table and fields.

Feel free add your valuable inputs.
